enrichment.short_description: Amberol is a free, open-source music player for the GNOME desktop that plays local audio files with a clean, minimal interface and deliberately no library management features.
enrichment.seo_title: Amberol 2026: Free Open-Source GNOME Music Player
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Amberol is a free, open-source GTK4 and Rust music player for GNOME and Linux. See its features, design philosophy, and how to install it in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Amberol is a free, open-source music player built specifically for the GNOME desktop environment on Linux. It is developed as part of the GNOME community's app ecosystem (distributed through apps.gnome.org and Flathub) and built using GTK4 and the Rust programming language, giving it a modern look that matches the visual language of contemporary GNOME applications, including gradient backgrounds that automatically tint themselves based on the artwork of the currently playing album or song. Amberol is not a commercial SaaS product and has no company behind it in the traditional sense; it is maintained by volunteer contributors within the GNOME World group on GNOME's GitLab instance, with releases such as the 2026.1 update continuing to refine the app. The defining characteristic of Amberol is its intentionally narrow scope: it plays music from local files and folders and nothing else. Users can drag and drop songs or folders into the app, control playback with standard transport controls (play, pause, skip, rewind, fast-forward), loop tracks, and reorder or manage a temporary play queue, and they can show or hide the playlist panel with a single click. However, Amberol deliberately does not offer library management, playlist saving, metadata editing, lyrics display, or artist/band information lookups -- features common in players like Rhythmbox, Clementine, or iTunes. This 'does one thing well' philosophy is explicitly stated by its developers as a design choice, aimed at users who want a lightweight, unintrusive player rather than a full media library manager. The recommended installation method is via Flatpak, which keeps the app sandboxed and consistent across Linux distributions, and it is also packaged in some distro repositories. Because it is fully open source (hosted on GNOME's GitLab under a free license), anyone can inspect, modify, fork, or contribute to the codebase; several community forks exist on GitHub. Amberol targets Linux desktop users, particularly those running GNOME-based distributions such as Fedora Workstation, Ubuntu (with GNOME), or Pop!_OS, who want a fast, simple, aesthetically pleasing way to play a personal local music collection without cloud sync, streaming integration, or complex library curation. It is best compared not to streaming apps like Spotify but to other minimalist local players; its main differentiator is its polish, GNOME Human Interface Guidelines compliance, and zero-configuration simplicity. There is no pricing, subscription, or paid tier of any kind since the software is entirely free and open source, funded by volunteer effort and the broader GNOME Foundation ecosystem rather than commercial revenue.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Amberol
paragraphs: Amberol is a free, open-source music player designed for the GNOME desktop environment on Linux. It is built with GTK4 and Rust, giving it a fast, native feel and a visual style consistent with modern GNOME applications.

Unlike full-featured media players, Amberol focuses on a single job: playing local audio files. It has no ambitions to become a music library manager, streaming client, or metadata editor, and its developers describe this narrow focus as an intentional design philosophy rather than a limitation.

The app is developed collaboratively within the GNOME community's 'World' app group and is distributed primarily through Flathub as a Flatpak, which makes installation consistent across different Linux distributions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Amberol supports drag-and-drop loading of individual songs or entire folders, immediate playback without configuration, and standard transport controls including play, pause, skip, rewind, and fast-forward.

Its interface dynamically tints its background gradient based on the artwork of the currently playing track, and the playlist/queue panel can be toggled on or off with a single click to keep the UI minimal.

The window is fully resizable to suit different workflows, and playback options include song looping and queue reordering, but Amberol deliberately omits saved playlists, tag/metadata editing, lyrics lookups, or artist biography panels.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Amberol is completely free and open source under a free software license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or in-app purchases of any kind.
It is maintained by volunteer contributors as part of the GNOME app ecosystem rather than by a commercial company, so there is no vendor to purchase licenses from.

features.title: Drag-and-drop playback
features.description: Load individual songs or entire folders instantly by dragging them into the app window, with no import or library-building step required.

features.title: Adaptive artwork-based theming
features.description: The player's background gradient automatically shifts color to match the artwork of the currently playing track.

features.title: Minimal playback controls
features.description: Play, pause, skip, rewind, fast-forward, and loop controls are available without deeper menus or configuration screens.

features.title: Collapsible queue panel
features.description: The playlist/queue view can be shown or hidden with a single click to keep the interface as small and unobtrusive as possible.

features.title: Resizable, GNOME-native UI
features.description: Built with GTK4 to follow GNOME Human Interface Guidelines, the window can be resized freely and adapts to GNOME's light and dark themes.

features.title: Flatpak distribution
features.description: Distributed primarily via Flathub, allowing sandboxed, consistent installation across virtually any Linux distribution.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Source code is hosted publicly on GNOME's GitLab, allowing anyone to inspect, fork, or contribute to the project.

pros: Completely free with no ads, tracking, or paid tiers
Lightweight and fast, with a clean GNOME-native interface
Simple drag-and-drop workflow requires no setup or configuration
Fully open source and auditable, with an active community of contributors
Attractive, dynamic UI that matches the GNOME desktop aesthetic
cons: No library management, so it cannot organize large music collections
No support for saved or smart playlists
No metadata/tag editing or lyrics display
Linux/GNOME-only, with no Windows, macOS, or mobile versions
Not suited for users who want streaming service integration

faqs.question: Is Amberol free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Amberol is entirely free and open source, with no subscriptions, license fees, or in-app purchases.

faqs.question: What platforms does Amberol run on?
faqs.answer: Amberol is built for the GNOME desktop environment on Linux and is distributed mainly through Flathub as a Flatpak.

faqs.question: Can Amberol manage or organize my whole music library?
faqs.answer: No. Amberol intentionally does not include library management, playlist saving, or tagging features; it only plays the files or folders you give it.

faqs.question: Does Amberol support streaming services like Spotify?
faqs.answer: No. Amberol only plays local audio files stored on your device; it has no streaming service integrations.

faqs.question: Can I edit song metadata or view lyrics in Amberol?
faqs.answer: No. Metadata editing and lyrics display are explicitly outside the scope of the app by design.

faqs.question: How do I install Amberol?
faqs.answer: The recommended method is installing the Flatpak package from Flathub, though some Linux distributions may also package it separately.

faqs.question: Is Amberol actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes. It continues to receive updates, such as the 2026.1 release, from GNOME community contributors.

use_cases.title: Casual local music playback
use_cases.description: Linux desktop users who keep a folder of MP3 or FLAC files and just want to play them without managing a library.

use_cases.title: Minimalist GNOME workflow
use_cases.description: Users who prefer lightweight, distraction-free apps that match the GNOME design language over full-featured media suites.

use_cases.title: Quick playback for developers and testers
use_cases.description: Developers who want a simple, no-frills way to test or play back local audio files during other work.

enrichment.short_description: Ambient.ai is an AI-powered physical security platform that uses computer vision on a company's existing camera and access-control infrastructure to detect and prioritize real-world threats in real time.
enrichment.seo_title: Ambient.ai 2026: AI Physical Security Platform Review
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ambient.ai turns existing security cameras into an AI threat-detection system. Explore its features, funding, pricing model, and enterprise use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ambient.ai is an enterprise physical security and surveillance intelligence company, founded in 2017 by Vikesh Khanna and Shikhar Shrestha and headquartered in Palo Alto, California. It should not be confused with 'Amberol,' an unrelated open-source Linux music player -- Ambient.ai is a venture-backed AI security company. The company's core product applies computer vision and, more recently, a proprietary vision-language model called Ambient Pulsar (described as an always-on reasoning model built specifically for physical security) to video feeds from an organization's existing camera infrastructure, meaning customers generally do not need to rip and replace their current camera hardware to adopt the platform. The platform is organized around several capability areas: Foundation, which provides real-time AI monitoring that flags relevant activity for security operations centers; Advanced Forensics, which reconstructs the sequence and causality of an incident by connecting people, places, and context; Access Intelligence, which correlates video with physical access control system (PACS) data to sharply cut down false alarms; and Threat Detection, which uses a library of over 150 verified threat signatures to catch anomalies such as tailgating, weapons, perimeter breaches, and unauthorized access. The company reports that the majority of alerts are resolved in under a minute and that combining video with access-control correlation eliminates the large majority of false alerts that traditionally overwhelm human security teams. Ambient.ai has raised roughly $146 million across multiple rounds, including Series A and Series B rounds led by Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) with participation from Y Combinator and SV Angel, and a $20 million strategic growth investment from Allegion Ventures (the venture arm of access-control giant Allegion) to accelerate adoption of AI-driven physical security. Ambient.ai serves large enterprises and institutions across sectors including corporate campuses, education, manufacturing, data centers, energy and utilities, healthcare, financial services, and museums, with publicly referenced customers including Adobe, TikTok, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, ServiceNow, and the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). Pricing is not publicly published and is quoted on a custom, enterprise-sales basis depending on the number of camera feeds, sites, and modules deployed, which is typical for this category of enterprise security software. Compared to traditional video management systems (VMS) or manual security monitoring, Ambient.ai's differentiator is layering an AI reasoning and alerting layer on top of existing camera and access-control investments rather than requiring new hardware, positioning it against other physical security AI vendors such as Verkada's AI features, Rhombus, and Evolv, though Ambient.ai emphasizes software-only integration with third-party camera and PACS vendors rather than selling its own cameras.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Ambient.ai
paragraphs: Ambient.ai is an AI-powered physical security intelligence platform for large enterprises, founded in 2017 by Vikesh Khanna and Shikhar Shrestha and based in Palo Alto, California.

Rather than requiring new cameras or sensors, Ambient.ai connects to a company's existing camera infrastructure and access-control systems, applying computer vision and a proprietary vision-language model (Ambient Pulsar) to detect and explain security-relevant events in real time.
The platform is aimed at enterprise security operations centers that are overwhelmed by manual video monitoring and high false-alarm rates from legacy systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Foundation provides always-on AI monitoring across camera feeds, automatically flagging activity relevant to security teams rather than requiring humans to watch every screen.
Advanced Forensics reconstructs incidents by connecting people, locations, and context, helping security teams understand what happened and why far faster than manual video review.

Access Intelligence correlates video footage with access control system data (badge swipes, door events) to dramatically reduce false alerts, and Threat Detection applies more than 150 verified threat signatures to catch specific risk patterns like tailgating or unauthorized entry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ambient.ai does not publish standard pricing; it sells to enterprise customers through a custom, quote-based sales process typically scoped around number of camera feeds, sites, and which product modules are deployed.
Prospective customers must contact Ambient.ai's sales team directly for a tailored quote and implementation plan.

features.title: Existing camera integration
features.description: Connects to a customer's current camera infrastructure and access-control systems rather than requiring new hardware.

features.title: Ambient Pulsar vision-language model
features.description: A proprietary, always-on AI reasoning model purpose-built for physical security video analysis.

features.title: Real-time threat detection
features.description: Applies more than 150 verified threat signatures to flag anomalies such as tailgating, weapons, and perimeter breaches.

features.title: Access Intelligence
features.description: Correlates video feeds with physical access control (PACS) data to cut down false alerts significantly.

features.title: Advanced Forensics
features.description: Reconstructs incident timelines by connecting people, places, and context for faster investigations.

features.title: Fast alert resolution
features.description: The company reports that the large majority of alerts are resolved by operators in under a minute.

features.title: Enterprise-scale deployment
features.description: Built for large, multi-site organizations across industries like data centers, healthcare, and financial services.

pros: Works with existing camera and access-control infrastructure, avoiding costly hardware replacement
Backed by substantial venture funding, including strategic investment from access-control leader Allegion
Reduces false alerts significantly through cross-referencing video and access data
Serves large, recognizable enterprise and institutional customers
Purpose-built AI model focused specifically on physical security use cases
cons: Pricing is not public and requires a custom enterprise sales process
Designed for large organizations, likely not cost-effective for small businesses
Effectiveness depends on the quality and coverage of a customer's existing camera network
As an AI surveillance tool, may raise privacy and governance considerations for some organizations

faqs.question: Is Ambient.ai the same company as Amberol?
faqs.answer: No. Ambient.ai is an AI physical security company for enterprises; Amberol is an unrelated open-source Linux music player. The names sound similar but the products have nothing in common.

faqs.question: Does Ambient.ai require new cameras?
faqs.answer: No. Ambient.ai is designed to integrate with a customer's existing camera and access-control infrastructure rather than requiring new hardware.

faqs.question: Who founded Ambient.ai and when?
faqs.answer: Ambient.ai was founded in 2017 by Vikesh Khanna and Shikhar Shrestha and is headquartered in Palo Alto, California.

faqs.question: How much does Ambient.ai cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is not publicly listed. Ambient.ai uses a custom, quote-based enterprise sales process based on deployment scope.

faqs.question: What industries use Ambient.ai?
faqs.answer: Customers span corporate campuses, education, manufacturing, data centers, energy and utilities, healthcare, financial services, and cultural institutions like museums.

faqs.question: How much funding has Ambient.ai raised?
faqs.answer: Ambient.ai has raised roughly $146 million across multiple rounds, including Series A and B rounds led by Andreessen Horowitz and a $20 million strategic investment from Allegion Ventures.

faqs.question: What is Ambient Pulsar?
faqs.answer: Ambient Pulsar is Ambient.ai's proprietary vision-language model, described as an always-on AI reasoning system built specifically for physical security applications.

use_cases.title: Enterprise security operations centers
use_cases.description: Large campuses use Ambient.ai to reduce the volume of camera feeds a human operator must monitor by having AI flag only relevant events.

use_cases.title: Access control anomaly detection
use_cases.description: Organizations correlate badge/access data with video to catch tailgating, unauthorized entry, and access violations with fewer false alarms.
use_cases.audience: Corporate and data center security teams

use_cases.title: Incident investigation and forensics
use_cases.description: Security and compliance teams use Advanced Forensics to reconstruct what happened during an incident faster than manual video review.

features: Scoped to number of camera feeds and sites
Access Intelligence and Threat Detection modules

enrichment.short_description: Ampt is a serverless-first cloud application platform that lets JavaScript and TypeScript developers build, deploy, and scale full-stack apps on AWS without managing infrastructure or configuration.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ampt is a serverless cloud platform for JavaScript and TypeScript apps on AWS. See its features, pricing tiers, and how it compares in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ampt, accessed at getampt.com, is a cloud application development platform built for JavaScript and TypeScript developers who want to build, deploy, and scale full-stack applications on AWS without hand-configuring infrastructure. Rather than requiring developers to manually set up services like API Gateway, Lambda, S3, or RDS, Ampt automatically provisions, manages, and optimizes the underlying AWS resources by inferring what the application needs directly from its code -- an approach the company describes as removing complicated configuration files and infrastructure management from the developer's workflow. Ampt provides a fully featured Node.js runtime with support for modern JavaScript and TypeScript, and it integrates with familiar backend frameworks including Express.js, Koa, Next.js, Astro, and Remix, making it approachable for teams already using standard JavaScript tooling. A signature feature is the developer sandbox: every individual developer gets an isolated cloud environment that syncs and deploys local code changes in under a second, enabling a live, iterative development loop directly against real cloud infrastructure rather than a local emulator. Ampt also offers Ampt SQL, a fully managed serverless Postgres database service built on Neon's technology, letting teams add relational data storage without provisioning or managing database servers themselves. The platform takes a scale-to-zero, serverless-first approach to deploying on AWS, meaning that low-traffic applications can often run for well under a dollar a month, since idle resources are not billed. Ampt's pricing is environment-based: a free 'Preview' tier lets developers explore the platform with one sandbox and one auto-pausing permanent stage under fixed resource quotas; the 'Developer' plan, at roughly $7 per month plus AWS usage, gives a single user three managed environments, CI/CD, preview environments, custom domains, and community support, with additional environments available a la carte; the 'Team' plan, at roughly $49 per month plus AWS usage, supports up to 15 team members with ten managed environments, password-protected environments, longer-running tasks, private Git repository support, and email support; and there are higher 'Self-Hosted' (starting around $500/month) and fully custom 'Scale' tiers for organizations that want Ampt deployed inside their own AWS accounts with dedicated support and compliance features. In all paid tiers, underlying AWS infrastructure usage is billed at standard AWS rates plus a 20 percent management fee on top. Ampt is positioned as a modern alternative to traditional Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) tools like Heroku or Vercel for developers who specifically want deep, native AWS integration combined with a serverless operating model, appealing most to startups and small-to-mid-size engineering teams building Node.js-based products who want to avoid dedicated DevOps overhead while still running on AWS infrastructure they ultimately own.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Ampt
paragraphs: Ampt (getampt.com) is a cloud application development platform that lets JavaScript and TypeScript developers build, deploy, and scale full-stack applications on AWS without manually configuring infrastructure.

The platform automatically provisions and manages cloud resources by inferring requirements directly from application code, combining a Node.js runtime, deployment engine, and account orchestration into a single developer experience.
Ampt supports popular backend frameworks such as Express.js, Koa, Next.js, Astro, and Remix, and includes Ampt SQL, a managed serverless Postgres service powered by Neon, for relational data needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every developer gets an isolated cloud sandbox environment that syncs local code changes and deploys them in under a second, enabling rapid iteration directly against live cloud infrastructure.
The platform is serverless-first and scale-to-zero by default, meaning idle or low-traffic applications typically cost well under a dollar a month in AWS usage.
Built-in CI/CD, preview environments, custom domain support, and (on paid plans) private Git repository integration and password-protected environments round out the developer workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ampt offers a free Preview tier with one sandbox and one auto-pausing stage, a Developer plan around $7/month plus AWS usage with three managed environments, and a Team plan around $49/month plus AWS usage supporting up to 15 members and ten managed environments.
Larger organizations can choose a Self-Hosted plan starting around $500/month for deployment inside their own AWS accounts, or a fully custom Scale plan for managed enterprise workloads.
On every paid tier, actual AWS resource consumption is billed at standard AWS rates plus a 20 percent Ampt management fee.

features.title: Automatic infrastructure provisioning
features.description: Ampt infers what cloud resources an application needs directly from its code, removing manual AWS configuration.

features.title: Sub-second developer sandboxes
features.description: Each developer gets an isolated cloud environment that syncs and deploys local changes in under a second.

features.title: Framework support
features.description: Works with Express.js, Koa, Next.js, Astro, and Remix on a fully featured Node.js and TypeScript runtime.

features.title: Ampt SQL (Neon-powered Postgres)
features.description: A fully managed serverless Postgres database service for relational data without server management.

features.title: Scale-to-zero serverless architecture
features.description: Applications automatically scale down when idle, keeping costs low for low-traffic apps.

features.title: Built-in CI/CD and preview environments
features.description: Deployment pipelines and preview environments are included out of the box for team collaboration.

features.title: Cost and usage reporting
features.description: Dashboards show AWS cost and usage breakdowns so teams can monitor infrastructure spend.

pros: Removes most manual AWS infrastructure configuration for JavaScript/TypeScript developers
Sub-second sandbox sync speeds up iterative development
Scale-to-zero pricing keeps costs very low for small or low-traffic apps
Supports popular frameworks like Next.js, Remix, Astro, and Express
Offers a free tier for exploring the platform before committing
cons: Primarily focused on the JavaScript/TypeScript/Node.js ecosystem, limiting use for other languages
Adds a 20 percent management fee on top of underlying AWS usage costs
Self-hosted and Scale tiers require significant budget for larger organizations
Less established track record and community size compared to major PaaS providers like Heroku or Vercel

faqs.question: What is Ampt used for?
faqs.answer: Ampt is a cloud application platform that lets JavaScript and TypeScript developers build, deploy, and scale full-stack apps on AWS without manually managing infrastructure.

faqs.question: Does Ampt have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ampt offers a free Preview tier with one sandbox environment and one auto-pausing permanent stage under fixed resource quotas.

faqs.question: How much does Ampt cost?
faqs.answer: The Developer plan starts around $7/month plus AWS usage, and the Team plan is around $49/month plus AWS usage; Self-Hosted and Scale plans are priced separately for larger organizations.

faqs.question: What frameworks does Ampt support?
faqs.answer: Ampt supports Node.js and TypeScript applications built with frameworks including Express.js, Koa, Next.js, Astro, and Remix.

faqs.question: Does Ampt include a database?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ampt SQL is a fully managed serverless Postgres database service powered by Neon, available directly within the platform.

faqs.question: Is Ampt built on AWS?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ampt provisions and manages AWS infrastructure automatically on the developer's behalf, and AWS usage is billed at standard rates plus a management fee.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Ampt in my own AWS account?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ampt offers a Self-Hosted plan, starting around $500/month, for deployment fully inside a customer's own AWS account.

use_cases.title: Rapid full-stack app development
use_cases.description: Solo developers and small teams build and deploy Node.js/TypeScript apps quickly without configuring AWS services manually.

use_cases.title: Team collaboration on cloud apps
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use isolated sandboxes, preview environments, and CI/CD to collaborate on shared cloud applications.

use_cases.title: Low-traffic and side projects

features: Fixed resource quotas on invocations, compute, bandwidth, and storage
Fully-managed workloads on customer AWS accounts

enrichment.short_description: Anaplan is an enterprise connected-planning platform used by large organizations for financial planning and analysis, supply chain planning, sales planning, and workforce planning on a single cloud data model.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Anaplan is an enterprise connected planning platform for FP&A, supply chain, and sales planning. See its history, features, pricing, and Thoma Bravo buyout.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Anaplan is an enterprise cloud software platform for connected business planning, spanning financial planning and analysis (FP&A), supply chain planning, sales performance planning, and workforce planning within a single unified data model. It was founded in 2006 by Guy Haddleton, Sue Haddleton, and Michael Gould, originating in a converted barn in Yorkshire, England, before the company relocated its headquarters to San Francisco, California, as it scaled globally. Anaplan built its platform around a proprietary in-memory calculation engine known as Hyperblock, which allows large organizations to model complex, interconnected business scenarios across departments in real time rather than relying on disconnected spreadsheets. The company went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker PLAN on October 12, 2018. In March 2022, private equity firm Thoma Bravo announced it would acquire Anaplan in an all-cash deal valued at approximately $10.7 billion (later renegotiated slightly downward to roughly $10.4 billion amid a dispute over compensation practices), taking the company private; the deal closed in mid-2022. As of its most recent public data, Anaplan employs roughly 2,700 people and serves more than 2,000 customers worldwide, including large enterprises across industries such as consumer goods, technology, retail, and financial services, with past public customer references including companies like Coca-Cola, Shell, and VMware. The platform today spans multiple planning domains: Finance modules support scenario planning, financial consolidation, and AI-driven analysis and reporting; Supply Chain modules cover demand planning, supply planning, and inventory optimization; Sales Planning modules use AI to align sales territories, quotas, and capacity to revenue opportunity; and Workforce Planning modules help align talent strategy and headcount budgets to business scenarios. Anaplan has increasingly layered AI-driven capabilities on top of its core planning engine, marketed under recent 'agentic enterprise' positioning to help organizations move faster from data to decisions. Anaplan does not publish standard pricing; sales are handled through a custom, quote-based enterprise process, with industry estimates suggesting typical annual contract values ranging from tens of thousands of dollars for smaller deployments up to seven figures annually for large, multi-module, multi-user enterprise rollouts, since cost is driven primarily by the number of users, the number of planning models, and which specific modules are licensed. As an enterprise-focused connected planning platform, Anaplan is typically compared to tools like Oracle EPM (formerly Hyperion), SAP Analytics Cloud, Workday Adaptive Planning, OneStream, and Jedox, and it is generally positioned toward large, complex organizations rather than small businesses, given its implementation cost and complexity.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Anaplan
paragraphs: Anaplan is an enterprise cloud platform for connected business planning, unifying financial planning, supply chain planning, sales planning, and workforce planning within a single shared data model, so that changes in one part of the business are reflected across every connected plan.

Founded in 2006 in Yorkshire, England by Guy Haddleton, Sue Haddleton, and Michael Gould, the company grew into a major enterprise software vendor headquartered in San Francisco, going public on the NYSE in 2018 under the ticker PLAN.

In 2022, private equity firm Thoma Bravo acquired Anaplan for approximately $10.7 billion, taking the company private; it continues to operate as a standalone enterprise planning vendor under Thoma Bravo ownership.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Anaplan's core technology is Hyperblock, a proprietary in-memory calculation engine that allows large, interconnected planning models to update and recalculate in real time as inputs change.

The platform offers purpose-built modules for FP&A (scenario planning, consolidation, reporting), supply chain planning (demand, supply, and inventory optimization), sales planning (territory and quota alignment), and workforce planning (headcount and talent budgeting).

More recently, Anaplan has added AI-driven capabilities across its modules to help planners generate scenarios, insights, and recommendations faster, under a broader 'agentic enterprise' decision-making positioning.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Anaplan does not publish list pricing publicly. Instead, the company uses a custom, quote-based enterprise sales process tailored to the number of users, planning models, and modules a customer licenses.
Industry estimates suggest annual contract values can range from roughly $30,000 to $50,000 for smaller deployments up to well over $1 million annually for large, multi-module enterprise rollouts.
Prospective customers must contact Anaplan's sales team or a certified partner directly to receive a tailored quote and implementation plan.

features.title: Hyperblock in-memory engine
features.description: A proprietary calculation engine that lets large, interconnected planning models recalculate in real time.

features.title: Connected planning across departments
features.description: Finance, supply chain, sales, and workforce plans share a single data model so changes propagate across the business.

features.title: Financial planning and analysis (FP&A)
features.description: Modules for scenario planning, financial consolidation, and AI-assisted reporting for finance teams.

features.title: Supply chain planning
features.description: Demand, supply, and inventory optimization modules for operations and supply chain teams.

features.title: Sales performance and territory planning
features.description: AI-driven tools to align sales territories, quotas, and capacity with revenue opportunity.

features.title: Workforce planning
features.description: Headcount and talent budget planning tied to business scenarios and financial plans.

features.title: AI-driven decision support
features.description: Recently added AI capabilities help generate scenarios and insights faster across planning modules.

features.title: Enterprise-grade scalability
features.description: Built to support large, complex organizations with thousands of users and interconnected planning models.

pros: Powerful connected-planning model that links finance, supply chain, sales, and workforce data
Real-time recalculation engine handles very large, complex models
Broad module coverage across major enterprise planning functions
Established enterprise vendor with a large customer base and long operating history
Increasingly incorporating AI-driven planning and forecasting capabilities
cons: Pricing is not public and requires a custom enterprise sales negotiation
Implementation can be complex and costly, often requiring certified consulting partners
Steeper learning curve than simpler planning or spreadsheet-based tools
Primarily suited to large enterprises, less accessible for small or mid-size businesses
Now privately held under Thoma Bravo, reducing public financial transparency

faqs.question: When was Anaplan founded?
faqs.answer: Anaplan was founded in 2006 in Yorkshire, England by Guy Haddleton, Sue Haddleton, and Michael Gould.

faqs.question: Is Anaplan still a public company?
faqs.answer: No. Anaplan went public on the NYSE in 2018 under the ticker PLAN but was acquired by private equity firm Thoma Bravo in 2022 for approximately $10.7 billion and is now privately held.

faqs.question: What is Anaplan used for?
faqs.answer: Anaplan is used for connected enterprise business planning, including financial planning and analysis, supply chain planning, sales planning, and workforce planning.

faqs.question: How much does Anaplan cost?
faqs.answer: Anaplan does not publish pricing publicly; it uses a custom, quote-based enterprise sales process, with estimated annual contracts ranging from tens of thousands to well over a million dollars depending on scope.

faqs.question: What is Hyperblock?
faqs.answer: Hyperblock is Anaplan's proprietary in-memory calculation engine that powers real-time recalculation of large, interconnected planning models.

faqs.question: Who are Anaplan's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Anaplan is typically compared to Oracle EPM, SAP Analytics Cloud, Workday Adaptive Planning, OneStream, and Jedox in the enterprise planning software category.

faqs.question: How many customers does Anaplan have?
faqs.answer: Anaplan reports serving more than 2,000 customers worldwide across industries including consumer goods, technology, retail, and financial services.

faqs.question: Where is Anaplan headquartered?
faqs.answer: Anaplan is headquartered in San Francisco, California, after originally being founded in Yorkshire, England.

use_cases.title: Enterprise financial planning and analysis
use_cases.description: Finance teams use Anaplan to run scenario planning, budgeting, forecasting, and consolidation across business units.

enrichment.short_description: Android Studio is Google's official, free integrated development environment (IDE) for building Android apps, built on the IntelliJ IDEA platform with an integrated emulator, Gradle build system, and design tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Android Studio 2026: Google's Free Android IDE Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Android Studio is Google's free, official IDE for Android development. Explore its emulator, Gradle build tools, design editor, and key features for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Android Studio is Google's official integrated development environment (IDE) for building native Android applications. First announced at Google I/O in 2013 and reaching a stable 1.0 release in December 2014, it replaced the earlier Eclipse-based Android Development Tools (ADT) as the primary way developers build Android apps. Android Studio is built on JetBrains' IntelliJ IDEA platform, inheriting IntelliJ's intelligent code editor, refactoring tools, and static analysis, while adding Android-specific tooling on top. The IDE is completely free to download and use, published by Google and maintained as part of the broader Android developer ecosystem, distributed at developer.android.com/studio for Windows, macOS, and Linux. Its core capabilities include an integrated Android Emulator that lets developers test apps across a wide range of virtual device configurations and API levels without needing physical hardware; deep integration with the Gradle build system for dependency management and build configuration; a visual Layout Editor and Theme Editor for building user interfaces, including support for Jetpack Compose, Google's modern declarative UI toolkit; an APK/AAB analyzer and App Profiler for inspecting performance, memory, CPU, and network usage; and built-in support for version control systems like Git. Android Studio supports both Java and Kotlin as primary languages for Android development, with Kotlin now Google's officially recommended language for new Android apps. Because it is free and comes directly from Google, Android Studio is the default choice for the vast majority of professional and hobbyist Android developers, and it is required (or strongly recommended) for accessing the latest Android SDK features, Play Store publishing tools, and Google's newest APIs as they are released. It has no subscription tiers, license fees, or paid upgrade path; Google monetizes the broader Android and Google Play ecosystem rather than the IDE itself. Compared to alternative approaches to Android development -- such as using IntelliJ IDEA Community Edition directly, VS Code with Flutter/React Native for cross-platform development, or older command-line-only workflows -- Android Studio remains the most complete, officially supported, and tightly integrated environment specifically for native Android app development, particularly for teams that need the latest emulator features, Compose previews, and Play Store publishing integrations on day one of a new Android OS or API release.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Android Studio
paragraphs: Android Studio is Google's official, free IDE for building native Android applications, first announced in 2013 and reaching a stable 1.0 release in late 2014 as the successor to the Eclipse-based ADT plugin.

It is built on the JetBrains IntelliJ IDEA platform, combining IntelliJ's proven code editor and refactoring tools with Android-specific features like an emulator, layout editor, and Gradle build integration.
As a Google product, Android Studio is the officially recommended and most widely used environment for developing apps for the Android operating system, supporting both Java and Kotlin.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The built-in Android Emulator lets developers test apps against a wide range of virtual devices and Android API levels without needing physical hardware.
Gradle is integrated as the default build system, handling dependency management, build variants, and multi-module project configuration directly within the IDE.

Design tools include a visual Layout Editor, Theme Editor, Asset Studio, and support for previewing Jetpack Compose UI, alongside performance tools like the App Profiler for inspecting CPU, memory, and network usage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Android Studio is completely free to download and use, with no subscription, license fee, or paid tier of any kind.
Google distributes it as part of the free Android developer toolchain, monetizing the broader Android and Google Play ecosystem rather than charging for the IDE itself.

features.title: Android Emulator
features.description: Test apps across a wide variety of virtual device configurations and Android API levels without physical hardware.

features.title: Gradle build system integration
features.description: Manages dependencies, build variants, and multi-module project builds directly inside the IDE.

features.title: IntelliJ-based code editor
features.description: Inherits intelligent code completion, refactoring, and static analysis from the JetBrains IntelliJ IDEA platform.

features.title: Visual Layout and Theme Editor
features.description: Build and preview user interfaces visually, including support for Jetpack Compose UI previews.

features.title: App Profiler
features.description: Inspect real-time CPU, memory, network, and energy usage to debug and optimize app performance.

features.title: Kotlin and Java support
features.description: First-class support for both Kotlin, Google's recommended language, and traditional Java development.

features.title: Version control integration
features.description: Built-in support for Git and other version control systems directly within the IDE workflow.

features.title: Play Store publishing tools
features.description: Tools for generating and analyzing AAB/APK bundles ready for Google Play Store submission.

pros: Completely free with no license fees or paid tiers
Official Google tool with day-one support for new Android OS and API releases
Comprehensive emulator eliminates the need for physical test devices
Deep IntelliJ-based code intelligence and refactoring tools
Integrated design tools support both traditional XML layouts and modern Jetpack Compose
cons: Can be resource-intensive, requiring a reasonably powerful computer for smooth performance
Steeper learning curve for developers new to Gradle and Android's project structure
Limited to native Android development, not suited for cross-platform iOS/Android projects
Emulator performance can be slow on lower-end hardware without virtualization support

faqs.question: Is Android Studio free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Android Studio is completely free to download and use, with no subscription or license fee.

faqs.question: What is Android Studio built on?
faqs.answer: Android Studio is built on the JetBrains IntelliJ IDEA platform, with Android-specific tools like an emulator and layout editor added on top.

faqs.question: What programming languages does Android Studio support?
faqs.answer: Android Studio primarily supports Kotlin, Google's recommended language for Android development, as well as Java.

faqs.question: Do I need a physical Android device to develop apps?
faqs.answer: No. Android Studio includes a built-in emulator that can simulate a wide range of Android devices and OS versions.

faqs.question: What build system does Android Studio use?
faqs.answer: Android Studio uses Gradle as its default build system for managing dependencies and build configurations.

faqs.question: When was Android Studio released?
faqs.answer: Android Studio was announced by Google in 2013 and reached a stable 1.0 release in December 2014.

faqs.question: Can Android Studio be used to publish apps to the Play Store?
faqs.answer: Yes. It includes tools for generating and analyzing AAB and APK bundles ready for submission to the Google Play Store.

faqs.question: Does Android Studio support Jetpack Compose?
faqs.answer: Yes. Android Studio includes design tools and live previews specifically for Jetpack Compose, Google's modern declarative UI toolkit.

use_cases.title: Native Android app development
use_cases.description: Developers build, test, and debug native Android applications in Kotlin or Java using the full IDE toolchain.

use_cases.title: App performance optimization
use_cases.description: Teams use the App Profiler to diagnose CPU, memory, and network issues before shipping to production.

use_cases.title: Learning Android development
use_cases.description: Students and hobbyists use the free IDE and emulator to learn Android app development without needing a physical device.

enrichment.short_description: AndroidFileHost is a free file hosting service historically used by the Android modding community to distribute large ROM, kernel, and firmware files, though most of its original download infrastructure is no longer functional.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AndroidFileHost was a free ROM and firmware file host for the Android modding community. See its history, current status, and community-run alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AndroidFileHost was a free file hosting website built specifically for the Android modding, custom ROM, and firmware community, closely associated with the XDA Developers forum ecosystem. For years it served as one of the primary places developers and enthusiasts uploaded large binary files -- custom ROMs, kernels, custom recoveries, GApps packages, and stock firmware images -- that were too large or too niche for general-purpose file hosts to support reliably and for free. The site was run independently by a long-time XDA community member rather than by a venture-backed company, and it built a reputation within the Android enthusiast community for being one of the few dependable, ad-light places to distribute large ROM-related files without hitting aggressive bandwidth caps or paywalls common on general consumer file lockers. Over the past several years, however, AndroidFileHost has become largely defunct: while the website itself remains online and its search/index pages are still browsable, most of the original download links no longer function, meaning a large volume of historical ROMs, kernels, recoveries, and mods that were hosted there are effectively inaccessible through the original site. In response, community members created independent archival efforts, most notably a project called AFHArchive, which crawled and preserved copies of files from AndroidFileHost before they disappeared, and some of the site's index now points to files preserved on the Internet Archive rather than serving files directly. There is no publicly documented founding company, funding history, or corporate ownership behind AndroidFileHost, consistent with its identity as a grassroots, community-operated service rather than a commercial SaaS business; there is also no indication of any paid tier ever having existed, as the service was always free to use for uploaders and downloaders alike. Because the platform's core download functionality has degraded significantly and it is not actively maintained in a meaningful way, it should be treated by users today primarily as a legacy resource: still worth checking or searching for historical files, but no longer a reliable primary destination for hosting new custom ROM or firmware releases, a role that has increasingly shifted to alternatives such as GitHub Releases, SourceForge, and dedicated archive mirrors within the XDA community.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AndroidFileHost
paragraphs: AndroidFileHost is a free file hosting service that became closely tied to the Android custom ROM and modding community, particularly users of the XDA Developers forums, as a place to upload and share large firmware, ROM, kernel, and recovery files.

It was run as an independent, community-oriented service rather than a funded commercial company, and it filled a niche that general consumer file hosts did not serve well: hosting very large binary files for a technical, hobbyist audience without aggressive paywalls.

In recent years the site has become largely non-functional for downloads, even though the website and its file index remain online, and community archival projects have stepped in to preserve at-risk files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features (Historical)
paragraphs: AndroidFileHost's site was organized around a searchable index of files by device, letting users browse ROMs, GApps packages, kernels, and firmware specific to particular Android device models.
The service was free for both uploading and downloading, without the download caps and forced waiting periods common on many general-purpose consumer file lockers.
Developer profile pages allowed known ROM and mod maintainers to organize their releases in one place, which became a trusted signal for reliability within the XDA community.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Current Status
paragraphs: AndroidFileHost was always free to use, with no subscription, paid tier, or premium account option publicly documented.

As of the most recent community reports, most original download links on the site no longer work, and the platform should be treated as a largely defunct legacy resource rather than an active, reliable hosting service.

features.title: Device-specific file index
features.description: Files were organized and searchable by specific Android device models, making it easy to find ROMs and firmware for a given phone.

features.title: Large binary file hosting
features.description: Supported hosting of large ROM, kernel, recovery, and firmware files that many general file hosts struggled to accommodate for free.

features.title: Developer profile pages
features.description: ROM and mod maintainers could organize their releases under dedicated profile pages within the site.

features.title: No aggressive paywalls or caps
features.description: Historically offered free downloads without the forced wait times and bandwidth limits common on consumer file lockers.

features.title: Community archival integration
features.description: Portions of the site's index now reference files preserved on the Internet Archive as original links have failed.

pros: Was completely free to use for both uploaders and downloaders
Became a trusted, central hub for Android ROM and firmware files within the modding community
Device-specific organization made it easy to find relevant files
No aggressive bandwidth caps or paywalls compared to general consumer file hosts
cons: Most original download links are no longer functional
No active corporate maintenance, support, or roadmap
Large amounts of historical ROM and firmware content are effectively lost or inaccessible
No transparency into ownership, funding, or long-term site plans

faqs.question: Is AndroidFileHost still working?
faqs.answer: The website remains online and browsable, but most of its original download links no longer function, so it is largely unreliable as an active file host today.

faqs.question: Was AndroidFileHost free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. AndroidFileHost was always a free service for both uploading and downloading files, with no documented paid tier.

faqs.question: What kind of files were hosted on AndroidFileHost?
faqs.answer: It primarily hosted large Android-related files such as custom ROMs, kernels, custom recoveries, GApps packages, and stock firmware images.

faqs.question: Who ran AndroidFileHost?
faqs.answer: It was operated independently by a long-time member of the XDA Developers community rather than by a funded commercial company, and no corporate ownership is publicly documented.

faqs.question: Is there an alternative to AndroidFileHost?
faqs.answer: Community-run archival projects like AFHArchive, along with platforms such as GitHub Releases and SourceForge, have increasingly taken over the role AndroidFileHost used to play.

faqs.question: Can I still find old ROMs that were hosted there?
faqs.answer: Some historical files have been preserved through community archival efforts and the Internet Archive, but many original uploads are no longer accessible.

faqs.question: Is AndroidFileHost affiliated with XDA Developers?
faqs.answer: It was closely associated with and popular within the XDA Developers community, but it operated as a separate, independently run service.

use_cases.title: Custom ROM distribution (historical)
use_cases.description: ROM developers uploaded and shared custom Android ROM builds for specific device models with the modding community.

use_cases.title: Firmware and recovery file archiving
use_cases.description: Users searched device-specific indexes to find stock firmware, kernels, and custom recovery images for repair or modding.

use_cases.title: Historical file recovery
use_cases.description: Researchers or enthusiasts today may check the site's remaining index or linked archives to locate legacy ROM and firmware files.

enrichment.short_description: AndroidIDE is a free, open-source mobile app that lets developers write, build, and run real Gradle-based Android applications directly on an Android device, without needing a desktop computer.
enrichment.seo_title: AndroidIDE 2026: Build Android Apps On Your Phone
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AndroidIDE lets you build real, Gradle-based Android apps directly on an Android device. Explore its features, open-source license, and current status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AndroidIDE is a free, open-source integrated development environment that runs directly on an Android device, allowing developers to write, build, and compile real Gradle-based Android applications entirely on their phone or tablet rather than on a desktop computer. It is a distinct project from Google's Android Studio, which is a desktop-only IDE; AndroidIDE instead targets the on-device, mobile-first development niche and is distributed by AndroidIDEOfficial, with source code hosted publicly on GitHub. AndroidIDE is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3) or later, meaning it is fully free and open source, with no cost to download or use. Under the hood, AndroidIDE provides a full Linux-like terminal environment on the device, built on top of the well-known Termux project, giving developers a genuine command-line experience for running build tools. It supports Gradle-based Android projects with minimal or no modification, meaning projects built for desktop Android Studio can, in many cases, be opened and compiled directly within AndroidIDE. The app bundles JDK 11 and JDK 17 for compiling Java and Kotlin code, provides API reference information for Android classes (including flags for deprecated, removed, or newly available APIs), and allows developers to export finished apps as Android App Bundles (AAB) suitable for publishing directly to the Google Play Store, all from an on-device workflow. This makes AndroidIDE particularly useful for developers in regions or situations where access to a laptop or desktop computer is limited or unavailable, for on-the-go bug fixes and small feature additions, or for hobbyists who want to learn Android development using only a phone. One important caveat for anyone evaluating AndroidIDE in 2026 is that the project is publicly documented as no longer actively maintained, meaning users should not expect new feature development, and may encounter compatibility issues with the newest Android Gradle Plugin or SDK versions going forward. As a free, open-source, community-built tool with no company, funding, or commercial pricing behind it, AndroidIDE occupies a unique niche compared to alternatives: it is the closest thing to a full native Android development environment that runs entirely on Android itself, distinct from cloud IDEs or remote-desktop-based mobile coding solutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AndroidIDE
paragraphs: AndroidIDE is a free, open-source app that turns an Android phone or tablet into a fully functional development environment for building real, Gradle-based Android applications, with no desktop computer required.
It is a separate and unrelated project from Google's Android Studio, which is a desktop-only IDE; AndroidIDE was built specifically to bring native Android app development on-device.
The project is distributed by AndroidIDEOfficial and licensed under GPLv3, with its full source code publicly available on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AndroidIDE includes a genuine Linux-like terminal environment, built on the Termux project, giving developers real command-line access to build tools directly on their device.
It supports Gradle-based Android Studio projects largely without modification, and bundles JDK 11 and JDK 17 for compiling both Java and Kotlin code.

Developers can view API reference details (including deprecated and newly available APIs) and export finished apps as Android App Bundles (AAB) ready for Google Play Store publishing, all from within the mobile app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Project Status
paragraphs: AndroidIDE is completely free and open source, distributed under the GNU General Public License v3 or later, with no paid tiers or in-app purchases.

As of the most recent public information, the project is documented as no longer actively maintained, so users should expect no further feature updates and possible compatibility gaps with newer Android build tooling.

features.title: On-device Gradle builds
features.description: Compiles real, Gradle-based Android Studio projects directly on an Android device with little to no modification needed.

features.title: Termux-based terminal
features.description: Provides a full Linux-like command-line environment on the device, built on the Termux project.

features.title: Bundled JDK 11 and JDK 17
features.description: Includes both Java Development Kit versions needed to compile modern Android Java and Kotlin code.

features.title: API reference lookups
features.description: Shows information about Android API classes and members, including deprecated, removed, and newly available flags.

features.title: AAB export for Play Store publishing
features.description: Lets developers export finished apps as Android App Bundles ready for direct submission to the Google Play Store.

features.title: Open-source, GPLv3-licensed codebase
features.description: Fully open source and free to inspect, modify, or fork, with source code hosted publicly on GitHub.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Enables real Android app development without needing a desktop or laptop computer
Supports genuine Gradle-based projects compatible with Android Studio
Includes a full Linux-like terminal for authentic command-line development
Useful for developers with limited access to desktop hardware
cons: The project is no longer actively maintained, so future compatibility is not guaranteed
Building large or complex projects on a phone's limited hardware can be slow
Lacks the polished visual design tools and debugging features of Android Studio
May struggle to keep pace with newer Android Gradle Plugin or SDK requirements over time

faqs.question: Is AndroidIDE the same as Android Studio?
faqs.answer: No. AndroidIDE is a separate, distinct open-source project that runs entirely on an Android device, while Android Studio is Google's official desktop-only IDE.

faqs.question: Is AndroidIDE free?
faqs.answer: Yes. AndroidIDE is completely free and open source, licensed under the GNU General Public License v3 or later.

faqs.question: Can AndroidIDE build real Android apps?
faqs.answer: Yes. It supports genuine Gradle-based Android Studio projects and can export finished apps as Android App Bundles ready for Play Store publishing.

faqs.question: Is AndroidIDE still being updated?
faqs.answer: As of the most recent public information, the project is documented as no longer actively maintained, so users should not expect new feature updates.

faqs.question: Does AndroidIDE include a terminal?
faqs.answer: Yes. It includes a full Linux-like terminal environment built on the Termux project for running command-line build tools.

faqs.question: What Java versions does AndroidIDE support?
faqs.answer: AndroidIDE bundles JDK 11 and JDK 17 for compiling Java and Kotlin-based Android projects.

faqs.question: Who develops AndroidIDE?
faqs.answer: AndroidIDE is developed by AndroidIDEOfficial as an open-source, community-driven project, with source code publicly available on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Mobile-only Android development
use_cases.description: Developers without access to a laptop or desktop can write, build, and test real Android apps directly on their phone.

use_cases.title: On-the-go bug fixes
use_cases.description: Existing app maintainers make small fixes or updates to their Android projects while away from a desktop setup.

use_cases.title: Learning Android development
use_cases.description: Students and hobbyists learn Gradle-based Android app development using only a smartphone.

enrichment.short_description: Angaza is a pay-as-you-go (PAYG) financing and IoT metering software platform that helps off-grid solar and appliance companies offer affordable, embedded financing to customers in emerging markets across Africa and Asia.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Angaza powers pay-as-you-go financing for off-grid solar and appliance companies in emerging markets. See its history, funding, features, and business model.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Angaza is a technology company that provides pay-as-you-go (PAYG) financing software for distributors of off-grid solar systems and other life-changing appliances, primarily serving last-mile markets across Africa and Asia. It was founded in 2010 by Stanford graduate Lesley Silverthorn Marincola, initially as a solar-light hardware company aimed at addressing energy poverty in rural, off-grid communities. In 2012, the company pivoted away from manufacturing its own solar-light hardware toward becoming a software and technology provider, building a PAYG metering and monitoring platform that other solar and appliance companies could use to finance their own products. The core of Angaza's technology is an embedded metering mechanism built into a distributor's hardware (such as a solar home system or solar water pump) that can remotely lock or unlock the device based on a customer's payment status, effectively turning otherwise unaffordable, high-upfront-cost products into small, manageable installment payments made over weeks or months via mobile money or cash. This embedded-finance model has allowed even very small, remote, last-mile distribution businesses to offer consumer financing they could not otherwise underwrite themselves, since Angaza's platform (rather than the distributor) manages payment tracking, credit risk data, and remote device control. Angaza is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with its technology deployed by distributor partners across more than 30 countries, predominantly in Sub-Saharan Africa (with early and continued strength in markets like Kenya and other East African countries) as well as parts of Asia. The company has raised meaningful venture funding over multiple rounds, including a $1.5 million seed round, a Series A round that combined to roughly $10.5 million, and a $13.5 million Series B round led by KawiSafi Ventures with participation from Total Carbon Neutrality Ventures and existing investors including Ajax Strategies, Emerson Collective, Rethink Impact, and Salesforce Ventures, reflecting strong interest from both climate-focused and mainstream venture investors in the off-grid energy access space. Angaza's platform and business model sit at the intersection of embedded finance, IoT-enabled hardware, and impact-oriented fintech, and the company is frequently referenced in energy-access and impact investing case studies (including by organizations like the Skoll Foundation and IMD business school) as a leading example of technology-enabled financial inclusion for underserved, cash-constrained populations. Pricing is not publicly listed, as Angaza sells its platform on a business-to-business basis to solar and appliance distribution companies rather than directly to consumers, with commercial terms customized to each distributor partner's scale and product line. Angaza is generally compared to other PAYG and off-grid solar technology enablers such as SolarisIndex platforms, Odyssey Energy Solutions, and PEG Africa's internal systems, though Angaza differentiates itself through its long operating history and its position as one of the earlier, more established software-only enablers in the space.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Angaza
paragraphs: Angaza is a technology company providing pay-as-you-go (PAYG) financing software to off-grid solar and appliance distribution companies, primarily serving last-mile customers across Africa and Asia.

Founded in 2010 by Lesley Silverthorn Marincola as a solar-light hardware company, Angaza pivoted in 2012 to become a software provider, building a metering and monitoring platform that other companies use to offer PAYG financing on their own products.
The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and its technology has been deployed by distribution partners in more than 30 countries, predominantly across Sub-Saharan Africa.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Angaza's core technology is an embedded metering mechanism built into a distributor's hardware, such as a solar home system or solar water pump, that can remotely lock or unlock the device based on customer payment status.
This embedded-finance approach allows customers to pay for expensive products in small, manageable installments over time via mobile money or cash, rather than requiring a large upfront purchase.

The platform manages payment tracking and credit-risk data for distributor partners, enabling even small, remote, last-mile businesses to offer financing they could not otherwise underwrite on their own.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Angaza does not publish consumer-facing pricing, since it sells its platform business-to-business to solar and appliance distribution companies rather than directly to end consumers.
Commercial terms are customized to each distributor partner based on scale, product line, and market, and interested distributors must contact Angaza directly for pricing.

features.title: Embedded PAYG metering
features.description: Hardware-embedded technology that remotely locks or unlocks solar and appliance products based on customer payment status.

features.title: Mobile money and cash payment support
features.description: Enables customers to make small, incremental payments via mobile money or cash rather than a large upfront cost.

features.title: Distributor-facing management platform
features.description: Gives last-mile distribution businesses tools to track payments, manage inventory, and monitor customer accounts.

features.title: Credit risk and payment data
features.description: Captures payment history data that helps distributors assess and manage customer credit risk over time.

features.title: Multi-country, multi-product support
features.description: Deployed across more than 30 countries and supports products ranging from solar home systems to solar water pumps.

features.title: Remote device control
features.description: IoT-enabled connectivity allows distributors to remotely manage financed devices in the field.

pros: Long operating history and established track record in the off-grid PAYG solar space since 2012
Backed by both climate-focused and mainstream venture investors
Enables small, last-mile distributors to offer financing they could not underwrite alone
Deployed at scale across more than 30 countries, mostly in Sub-Saharan Africa
Embedded metering technology directly addresses affordability barriers for off-grid customers
cons: Pricing is not publicly available and requires direct contact with sales
Serves distribution businesses (B2B) rather than end consumers directly, adding a layer of complexity for smaller solar companies
Success depends heavily on distributor partners' own operational execution and market conditions
Primarily focused on emerging markets, less relevant outside the off-grid and energy access sector

faqs.question: What does Angaza do?
faqs.answer: Angaza provides pay-as-you-go (PAYG) financing software that helps off-grid solar and appliance companies offer affordable, installment-based financing to customers in emerging markets.

faqs.question: When was Angaza founded?
faqs.answer: Angaza was founded in 2010 by Lesley Silverthorn Marincola, originally as a solar-light hardware company before pivoting to software in 2012.

faqs.question: How does Angaza's pay-as-you-go technology work?
faqs.answer: Angaza's platform uses an embedded metering mechanism in a distributor's hardware that remotely locks or unlocks the device based on whether the customer has made their payment.

faqs.question: Where does Angaza operate?
faqs.answer: Angaza's technology is deployed by distributor partners in more than 30 countries, predominantly across Sub-Saharan Africa, as well as parts of Asia.

faqs.question: Is Angaza a hardware or software company?
faqs.answer: Angaza is now a software and technology company; it started as a solar-light hardware maker in 2010 but pivoted to providing PAYG software to other hardware distributors in 2012.

faqs.question: Who are Angaza's customers?
faqs.answer: Angaza sells to off-grid solar and appliance distribution companies (a B2B model), not directly to individual end consumers.

faqs.question: How much funding has Angaza raised?
faqs.answer: Angaza has raised multiple rounds including a $1.5 million seed round, roughly $10.5 million in Series A funding, and a $13.5 million Series B round led by KawiSafi Ventures.

faqs.question: Where is Angaza headquartered?
faqs.answer: Angaza is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

use_cases.title: Off-grid solar home system financing
use_cases.description: Solar distribution companies use Angaza's platform to offer customers small solar systems on a pay-as-you-go installment basis.

use_cases.title: Solar water pump financing
use_cases.description: Agricultural equipment distributors use Angaza's embedded metering technology to finance solar-powered irrigation pumps for farmers.

use_cases.title: Last-mile appliance financing
use_cases.description: Small, remote distribution businesses use Angaza's platform to extend financing on other life-changing appliances to underserved customers.

features: Distributor payment and inventory management platform
